entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion and publication workflow, supplied through the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00119
notes: The file is 33,200 bytes; IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID all identify CME00119.
The root text declares LANG="eng enm" and the 2016 TEMPHEAD entry records MURP proofing and structural adjustment.
The XML has three numbered DIV2 selections, 78 seven-line LG elements, 546 L elements, 14 PB elements spanning printed pages 740–753, and 11 NOTE elements.

rights.basis: The pinned CME repository root LICENSE is the Unlicense but expressly describes software and does not identify XML data or this file's markup as covered. The license is therefore retained as evidence without broadening it to the artifact.

access.accessMethod: Current publisher article landing page and DOI; bibliographic metadata is public, full publisher text requires entitlement.
access.entityLabel: Kingsford, Extracts from the First Version of Hardyng's Chronicle, 1912

bibliographic.citation: C. L. Kingsford, ‘Extracts from the First Version of Hardyng's Chronicle,’ The English Historical Review 27, no. 108 (October 1912): 740–753, https://doi.org/10.1093/ehr/XXVII.CVIII.740.
bibliographic.title: Extracts from the First Version of Hardyng's Chronicle

entities.description: The immediate source edition: a selective transcription of three discontiguous passages from Lansdowne MS 204, with Kingsford's short preface, historical notes, damage restorations, limited later-version comparisons, and tailnote.
entities.label: Kingsford, Extracts from the First Version of Hardyng's Chronicle, 1912
notes: The article prints the introduction, praise of Henry V, and conclusion in full as Kingsford defined those three selections; it does not print the intervening Chronicle.
Kingsford's final locator says f. 226v, while current BL foliation and images place the envoy on f. 226r and the map on f. 226v.

rights.basis: The article was published in 1912, before the current U.S. public-domain threshold for published works stated in Copyright Office Circular 15A.
rights.component: 1912 published article, transcription, preface, and editorial notes
notes: Current OUP access controls do not alter the U.S. copyright status of the 1912 article.

access.accessMethod: Current DOI and publisher platform; bibliographic metadata is public, full publisher text requires entitlement.
access.entityLabel: Kingsford, The First Version of Hardyng's Chronicle, 1912

bibliographic.citation: C. L. Kingsford, ‘The First Version of Hardyng's Chronicle,’ The English Historical Review 27, no. 107 (July 1912): 462–482, https://doi.org/10.1093/ehr/XXVII.CVII.462.
bibliographic.title: The First Version of Hardyng's Chronicle

entities.description: Kingsford's preceding analytical article on Lansdowne MS 204, explicitly referenced by the October extracts article as its first installment and explanatory context.
entities.label: Kingsford, The First Version of Hardyng's Chronicle, 1912
notes: This article analyzes the manuscript and promises the three extracts published in the next issue; it is contextual scholarship, not the immediate transcription source for the repository XML.

rights.basis: The article was published in 1912, before the current U.S. public-domain threshold for published works stated in Copyright Office Circular 15A.
rights.component: 1912 published analytical article

dateStatements.notes: The official catalog gives this object-production range.

dateStatements.label: modern edition production context
dateStatements.notes: This scholarly interpretation is preserved separately from the current catalog's 1440–1450 range.
dateStatements.value: 1450s, probably as a presentation manuscript completed in 1457

entities.description: The sole surviving manuscript of Hardyng's first-version Chronicle and the direct base witness for Kingsford's selection.

identifiers.scheme: British Library Archives and Manuscripts record

entities.label: British Library Lansdowne MS 204
notes: The catalog calls this the sole copy of the first version and distinguishes at least sixteen later-version copies.
Current target loci are ff. 2v–4r, f. 216r final stanza through f. 217v first stanza, f. 220r final stanza through f. 223v first three stanzas, and f. 226r final two stanzas.

physicalDescription.extent: 230 folios, with two unfoliated paper flyleaves at the beginning and two at the end
notes: Gothic cursive in at least two hands; Hand 1 writes ff. 5r–225v and marginalia, while Hand 2 writes f. 2v, ff. 3r–4r, f. 4v, ff. 226r–230v, headings, and other additions.
Middle English and Latin; produced in eastern England, probably Lincolnshire or East Anglia.
Contents include dedication f. 2v, prologue ff. 3r–4r, incomplete table f. 4v, Books 1–7 ff. 5r–230v, Umfraville arms f. 220r, map ff. 226v–227r, and Latin documents ff. 227v–230v.

rights.basis: The medieval text was created in the fifteenth century and is far outside current U.S. copyright terms; this claim concerns the historical text, not modern editorial matter or images.
rights.component: fifteenth-century Hardyng text selected in CME00119

rights.basis: Ownership and access to the physical manuscript are not themselves copyright in the medieval text; consultation remains subject to institutional access conditions.
rights.component: physical ownership, onsite consultation, and request administration

rights.basis: Current imaging-service terms make supply and reuse subject to service conditions and written permission; any third-party rights remain separate.
rights.component: newly supplied photography or reproduction files and permission to reproduce them
rights.jurisdiction: British Library imaging-service terms; reuse jurisdiction-dependent

access.accessMethod: Public IIIF Presentation 3 manifest, Universal Viewer, and unauthenticated JPEG image requests.
access.entityLabel: British Library complete IIIF facsimile of Lansdowne MS 204
notes: No local copy was made. The 474-canvas object is publicly readable, but the manifest's manuscript-image reuse link returned HTTP 404 and current BL terms do not establish permission to bulk-cache and recirculate the complete image set.
This is a deliberate no-cache decision for a publicly available resource, not a claim that the facsimile is unavailable.

The tested target boundaries are canvases 10–13 (ff. 2v–4r), 437–440 (f. 216r final stanza through f. 217v first stanza), 445–452 (f. 220r final stanza through f. 223v first three stanzas), canvas 457 (f. 226r final two stanzas), and canvas 458 (map on f. 226v).

rights.basis: The IIIF manifest exposes public images but points to an item-level manuscript-image reuse page that currently returns HTTP 404. Current general BL terms apply, but no clear open image license or bulk-cache permission was found.
rights.component: existing British Library IIIF manuscript photographs and image services
rights.jurisdiction: British Library provider terms; reuse jurisdiction-dependent
notes: Public availability is recorded separately from the unresolved reuse position; the images were not bulk-cached.

entities.description: A public IIIF Presentation 3 object of 474 canvases, from front cover through spine, including all folios and flyleaves.
entities.label: British Library complete IIIF facsimile of Lansdowne MS 204
notes: The complete public object was audited but not bulk-cached; the access record documents the separate no-cache decision.
The target canvases are 10–13, 437–440, 445–452, and 457. Canvas 458 shows the map on f. 226v.

bibliographic.citation: John Hardyng, John Hardyng's Chronicle, Edited from British Library MS Lansdowne 204, vol. 1, ed. James Simpson and Sarah Peverley (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 2015).
bibliographic.title: John Hardyng's Chronicle, Edited from British Library MS Lansdowne 204, Volume I

entities.description: The first published volume of a modern edition from Lansdowne MS 204. It includes the scholarly introduction, dedication, prologue, table, and Books 1–3, but not the Book 7 praise and conclusion corresponding to most of Kingsford's extracts.
entities.label: Simpson and Peverley, John Hardyng's Chronicle, Volume I, 2015
notes: The edition recovers erased Prol.155–161 under ultraviolet light; Kingsford and CME00119 end the prologue at Prol.154.
The project-level description of a complete edition must not be read as proof that the currently available Volume I contains all seven books.

rights.basis: METS states that digital editions are for individual use and directs requests to reprint to the project; public readability is not an open license for the modern edition.

entities.description: A Chronicle-level verse record identifying Hardyng, seven-line ababbcc stanzas, twenty witness entries across both versions, and Lansdowne wit-1174-10 at ff. 5–226; it catalogs Kingsford pp. 740–753 as extracts only.
entities.label: DIMEV v1.0 record 1174 / IMEV 710
notes: The active record does not identify Kingsford's three selections as separate works; a dedication record appears only as commented-out source data.
DIMEV's bibliography pairs Kingsford's first-article title with pp. 740–753, but the article itself and OUP metadata establish the immediate article's exact Extracts title.

rights.basis: The tagged v1.0 archive includes a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International license.

next_steps: Seek an explicit repository statement identifying whether XML data and markup are covered components.
Until clarified, keep the artifact status unknown and do not treat U-M's NoC-US statement as a worldwide open-data license.
openQuestions.question: Does the CME submodule's software-worded Unlicense apply to CME00119 XML data and markup?

next_steps: Ask the University of Michigan Library for item-level production metadata and any preserved source package for CME00119.
Compare any supplied source with the October 1912 issue and checked-in XML without treating the 2016 review as the initial encoding date.
openQuestions.question: What initial keying source, date, transcription method, correction history, and bulk-source package preceded the repository XML's documented 2016 MURP review?

next_steps: Ask the British Library whether the manuscript was refoliated or whether Kingsford's side designation is simply an error.
Retain both the historical printed locator and current verified folio/canvas in discovery data.
openQuestions.question: Why does Kingsford locate the final two stanzas at f. 226v when current British Library foliation and images place them on f. 226r and the map on f. 226v?

next_steps: Ask METS or the editors for the status of Volume II and whether a stable critical text or line mapping for Book 7 is available.
Do not imply that the cached 2015 Volume I contains the conclusion merely because the project description discusses a complete multivolume edition.
openQuestions.question: What is the current publication status of the promised later METS volume containing Book 7 and the complete modern text corresponding to Kingsford's praise and conclusion extracts?

next_steps: Ask the British Library to identify the replacement item-level rights route and whether complete IIIF bulk caching and repository recirculation are permitted.
Keep the facsimile status publicly_available but omit local copies until permission or a clear open license is documented.
openQuestions.question: What current item-level terms govern bulk caching and redistribution of the public Lansdowne MS 204 IIIF images now that the manifest's manuscript-image reuse link returns HTTP 404?

relations.objectLabel: Kingsford, Extracts from the First Version of Hardyng's Chronicle, 1912

subject.description: bibliography and Lansdowne witness edition note

reviewNotes: The primary subject is the checked-in XML artifact; the cataloged intellectual unit is Kingsford's edited selection, not the complete first-version Chronicle, manuscript, or journal issue.
Lansdowne MS 204 is the sole direct first-version witness. Ellis's 1812 later-version edition is cited only for passage-level comparisons and is not a base witness.
The three numbered XML units are substantive verse selections. Their embedded manuscript-derived arguments and rubrics are paratext, not extra numbered works.
The modern METS Volume I PDF contains the introduction, dedication, prologue, and Books 1–3; it does not contain the Book 7 extracts printed by Kingsford.
Public BL image delivery and permission to bulk-cache or redistribute those images are distinct questions; the absence of a cache is not an availability claim.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a pinned copy of University of Michigan CME item CME00119, which encodes C. L. Kingsford's October 1912 article and selective transcription of three discontiguous passages from John Hardyng's first-version Chronicle. Kingsford transcribed the sole first-version witness, British Library Lansdowne MS 204, rather than Ellis's 1812 later-version edition; Ellis supplies only limited comparison passages. The selection comprises the dedication and most of the prologue, praise of Henry V, and a composite conclusion, plus Kingsford's preface and tailnote. A complete Internet Archive scan and OCR of each Kingsford issue, a complete Ellis scan, the complete METS 2015 Volume I PDF, and DIMEV v1.0 are cached with exact work locators. The British Library's 474-canvas IIIF object is publicly readable but deliberately not cached because its item-level reuse link is dead and current provider terms do not establish permission for bulk recirculation.

catalogSummary.author: John Hardyng
catalogSummary.date: First-version Chronicle completed in 1457; edited selection published 1 October 1912
catalogSummary.region: England; exact place of composition is not securely established; the sole manuscript was produced in eastern England
catalogSummary.title: Extracts from the First Version of Hardyng's Chronicle

catalogingSubject.label: Extracts from the First Version of Hardyng's Chronicle
catalogingSubject.scope_note: C. L. Kingsford's October 1912 editorial selection of three discontiguous passages from John Hardyng's first-version Chronicle: the dedication and most of the prologue, the praise of Henry V, and a composite conclusion comprising praise of Robert Umfraville, counsel on common profit, Hardyng's grievance, and two final envoy stanzas. The cataloged unit includes Kingsford's short preface and tailnote as explicit editorial components but does not turn them into Hardyng's authorship. It excludes the intervening Scotland-itinerary chapter summarized but not transcribed on p. 753, the rest of Hardyng's Chronicle, the physical manuscript, the journal issue as a carrier, the U-M encoding, and the repository XML as distinct layers.

Kingsford notes that two stanzas have slight-variation parallels in Ellis's later-version edition, p. 388; this does not make the later version a base witness.
contentParts.summary: Fourteen stanzas lament Henry V, praise his governance, peacekeeping, and French campaigns, and turn that example into direct counsel to Henry VI against disorder and for recovery in France.

contentParts.summary: Eleven stanzas describe Hardyng's intelligence work in Scotland, his claimed costs and injury, Henry V's promise, Henry VI's frustrated grant of Geddington, and the unrewarded evidence offered to the crown.

contentParts.summary: Two final rhyme-royal stanzas ask Henry VI to accept the book and service, consider Hardyng's loss and injury, and excuse his limited literary ability.

assertion.notes: The 1450–1457 range is an operational summary of current scholarship, not a claim that every selected stanza was first drafted in that span. Kingsford argued that the praise of Henry V may predate the loss of Normandy, but that passage-specific inference is not normalized as a separate firm date.

dateStatements.display: Composed and revised principally in the 1450s; completed in 1457

scope.description: The selected passages as parts of Hardyng's first-version Chronicle
Kingsford's selection, transcription, arrangement, preface, and notes

extent.description: Parent-aware encoding metrics. The three top-level numbered verse selections contain 78 seven-line stanzas and 546 lines. Seven leaf-level verse components partition that total without overlap; two prose-only editorial components are counted separately. Four unnumbered manuscript argument or rubric paragraphs are embedded within the numbered selections, and one further rubric for the omitted Scotland chapter is quoted inside Kingsford's tailnote; these paratexts are not additional numbered works or prose items. Introduction and Conclusion are aggregate parent containers and are not added again to the leaf-level item totals. Eleven modeled content parts include those two aggregate parents.

assertion.notes: The mixed label describes the cataloged 1912 selection. Hardyng's intrinsic selected text is verse; modern prose does not alter the form of the medieval Chronicle.

formStatements.description: An edited selection combining 546 Middle English verse lines in seventy-eight seven-line stanzas with Kingsford's prose preface, tailnote, arguments, and annotations.

scope.description: Kingsford's modern article framing and notes

notes: Lansdowne MS 204 is the sole first-version witness. Ellis 1812 is cited for limited later-version parallels and the omitted itinerary, not silently promoted to a second base witness.
The cataloged military and sovereignty claims are historical subject matter and are not endorsed.
All stanza and line totals are reproducible encoding metrics from the pinned XML. Parent aggregates are explicitly non-additive.

The current BL catalog's 1440–1450 manuscript range, the modern edition's 1450s production account, and the 1457 completion/presentation claim are kept as distinct assertions rather than forced into one date.

next_steps: Ask the University of Michigan Library for item-level production metadata and any preserved source package for CME00119.
Compare any supplied source with the October 1912 issue and the checked-in XML without treating the 2016 review as the initial encoding date.
openQuestions.question: What initial keying source, date, transcription method, correction history, and bulk-source package preceded the repository XML's documented 2016 MURP review?

next_steps: Ask the British Library whether the manuscript was refoliated or whether Kingsford's side designation is simply an error.
Retain both the historical printed locator and current verified folio/canvas in discovery data.
openQuestions.question: Why does Kingsford locate the final two stanzas at f. 226v when the current British Library foliation and viewer place them on f. 226r and the map on f. 226v?

next_steps: Ask METS or the editors for the status of Volume II and whether a stable critical text or line mapping for Book 7 is available.
Do not imply that the cached 2015 Volume I contains the Kingsford conclusion merely because its landing page describes the wider multivolume project as a complete edition.
openQuestions.question: What is the current publication status of the promised later METS volume containing Book 7 and the complete modern critical text corresponding to Kingsford's praise and conclusion extracts?

assertion.notes: The eastern-English production of Lansdowne 204 is a witness fact, not proof of the exact place where Hardyng composed every selected passage.

Commemoration and praise of Henry V
Authorial grievance, service, injury, and hoped-for reward

summaries.value: Kingsford selected three politically and autobiographically distinctive passages from Hardyng's first-version Chronicle. The dedication and proem present the book to Henry VI while rehearsing Hardyng's Scottish evidence and unrewarded service; the praise of Henry V turns royal commemoration into counsel about peace and law; and the conclusion praises Robert Umfraville, urges the king to defend common profit, renews Hardyng's grievance, and closes with two envoy stanzas. The selection is a direct transcription from the sole first-version witness, not a multi-witness critical edition.

The XML contains one prose editorial preface and three numbered verse selections. Its 78 LG elements each hold seven lines, for 546 verse lines: Introduction 22 stanzas/154 lines (Dedication 4/28; Proem 18/126), Praise of Henry V 14/98, and Conclusion 42/294 (Umfraville 15/105; common-profit counsel 14/98; grievance 11/77; quoted envoy 2/14). Kingsford's prose tailnote summarizes but does not transcribe the intervening Scotland chapter.

The manuscript run is discontiguous: dedication/proem at ff. 2v–4r; praise of Henry V at ff. 216r–217v; conclusion main run at ff. 220r–223v; and final envoy at current f. 226r. The article omits the text between the third stanza on f. 223v and the final two stanzas on f. 226r. Kingsford prints the envoy locator as f. 226v, but the current BL viewer shows f. 226v occupied by the Scotland map and the envoy on f. 226r.

Kingsford and the XML print 154 lines for the dedication and proem. Simpson and Peverley recover a further erased seven-line stanza, Prol.155–161, under ultraviolet light at the bottom of f. 4r. The complete manuscript prologue is therefore 161 lines, while this historical 1912 selection legitimately remains 154 lines.

work.summary: Kingsford selected three politically and autobiographically distinctive passages from Hardyng's first-version Chronicle. The dedication and proem present the book to Henry VI while rehearsing Hardyng's Scottish evidence and unrewarded service; the praise of Henry V turns royal commemoration into counsel about peace and law; and the conclusion praises Robert Umfraville, urges the king to defend common profit, renews Hardyng's grievance, and closes with two envoy stanzas. The selection is a direct transcription from the sole first-version witness, not a multi-witness critical edition.
